passages.title: Inside the mind of a fan
passages.source_html:
Inside the mind of a fan


How watching sport affects the brain


A


At about the same time that the poet Homerigo invented the epic hero, the ancient Greeks started a festival in which men competed in a single race, about 200 metres long. The winner received a branch of wild olives. The Greeks called this celebration the Olympics. Though the ancient sprint remains, today the Olympics are far more than that. Indeed, the Games seem to celebrate the dream of progress as embodied in the human form. That the Games are intoxicating to watch is beyond question. During the Athens Olympics in 2004, 3.4 billion people, half the world, watched them on television. Certainly, being a spectator is a thrilling experience: but why?


B


In 1996, three Italian neuroscientists Giacomo Rizzolatti, Leonardo Fogassi and Vittorio Gallese, examined the premotor cortex of monkeys. They discovered that inside these primate brains there were groups of cells that 'store vocabularies of motor actions'. Just as there are grammars of language, rules for forming a sentence, there are grammars of movement. These networks of cells are the bodily 'sentences' we use every day, the ones our brain has chosen to retain and refine. Think, for example, about a golf swing. To those who have only watched the Masters' Tournament on TV, golfing seems easy. To the novice, however, the skill of casting a smooth arc with a lop-sided metal stick is virtually impossible. This is because most novices swing with their consciousness, using an area of brain next to the premotor cortex. To the expert, on the other hand, a perfectly balanced stroke is second nature. For him, the motor action has become memorised, and the movements are embedded in the neurons of his premotor cortex. He hits the ball with the tranquillity of his unconscious, his neurons on a perfected autopilot.


C


These neurons in the premotor cortex, besides explaining why certain athletes seem to possess almost unbelievable levels of skill, have an even more amazing characteristic, one that caused Rizzolatti, Fogassi and Gallese to give them the lofty title 'mirror neurons'. They note, 'The main functional characteristic of mirror neurons is that they become active both when the monkey performs a particular action (for example, grasping an object or holding it) and, astonishingly, when it sees another individual performing a similar action.' Humans have an even more elaborate mirror neuron system. These peculiar cells mirror, inside the brain, the outside world: they enable us to internalise the actions of another. In order to be activated, though, these cells require what the scientists call 'goal-orientated movements'. If we are staring at a photograph, a fixed image of a runner mid-stride, our mirror neurons are totally silent. They only fire when the runner is active: running, moving or sprinting.


D


What these electrophysiological studies indicate is that when we watch a golfer or a runner in action, the mirror neurons in our own premotor cortex light up as if we were the ones competing. This phenomenon of neural mirroring was first discovered in 1954, when two French physiologists, Gastaut and Bert, found that the brains of humans vibrate with two distinct wavelengths, alpha and mu. The mu system is involved in neural mirroring. It is active when our bodies are still, and disappears whenever we do something active, like playing sport or changing the TV channel. The surprising fact is that the mu signal is also quiet when we watch someone else being active, as on TV. These results are the effect of mirror neurons.


E


Rizzolatti, Fogassi and Gallese call the idea of mirror neurons the 'direct matching hypothesis'. They believe that we only understand the movement of sports stars when we 'map the visual representation of the observed action onto our motor representation of the same action'. According to this theory, watching an Olympic athlete 'causes the motor system of the observer to resonate ... The "motor knowledge" of the observer is used to understand the observed action.' But mirror neurons are more than just the neural basis for our attitude to sport. It turns out that watching a great golfer makes us better golfers, and watching a great sprinter actually makes us run faster. This ability to learn by watching is a crucial skill. From the acquisition of language as infants to learning facial expressions, mimesis (copying) is an essential part of being conscious. The best athletes are those with a premotor cortex capable of imagining the movements of victory, together with the physical properties to make those movements real.


F


But how many of us regularly watch sports in order to be a better athlete? Rather, we watch sport for the feeling, the human drama. This feeling also derives from mirror neurons. By letting spectators share in the motions of victory, they also allow us to share in its feelings. This is because they are directly connected to the amygdala, one of the main brain regions involved in emotion. During the Olympics, the mirror neurons of whole nations will be electrically identical, their athletes causing spectators to feel, just for a second or two, the same thing. Watching sports brings people together. Most of us will never run a mile in under four minutes, or hit a home run. Our consolation comes in watching: when we gather round the TV, we all feel, just for a moment, what it is to do something perfectly.

groups.title: Questions 27-40
groups.source_html:
Questions 27 - 32


Look at the six paragraphs (A - F). Which paragraph contains the following information?

You may choose any letter more than once.

Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
27

27 an explanation of why watching sport may be emotionally satisfying
✅ 正确答案：F

定位信息：watching sport, emotionally satisfying


对应原文：F段，"By letting spectators share in the motions of victory, they also allow us to share in its feelings." 以及"Our consolation comes in watching: when we gather round the TV, we all feel, just for a moment, what it is to do something perfectly. "


解题思路：F段落提到镜像神经元能使观众分享胜利的感受，因此答案是F。
28

28 an explanation of why beginners find sporting tasks difficult
✅ 正确答案：B

定位信息：beginners, sporting tasks, difficult


对应原文：B段，" Think, for example, about a golf swing. To those who have only watched the Masters' Tournament on TV, golfing seems easy. To the novice, however, the skill of casting a smooth arc with a lop-sided metal stick is virtually impossible. This is because most novices swing with their consciousness, using an area of brain next to the premotor cortex. To the expert, on the other hand, a perfectly balanced stroke is second nature."


解题思路：本题询问为什么初学者发现运动任务困难，B段提到说初学者刻意控制挥杆，而职业运动员控制挥杆已经变为了一种本能。因此答案是B。
29

29 a factor that needs to combine with mirroring to attain sporting excellence
✅ 正确答案：E

定位信息：combine, mirroring, attain sporting excellence


对应原文：E段，"The best athletes are those with a premotor cortex capable of imagining the movements of victory, together with the physical properties to make those movements real."


解题思路：E段提到除了镜像神经元，运动员还需要有身体能力完成动作，因此答案是E。
30

30 a comparison of human and animal mirror neurons
✅ 正确答案：C

定位信息：comparison, human, animal


对应原文：C段，" They note, 'The main functional characteristic of mirror neurons is that they become active both when the monkey performs a particular action (for example, grasping an object or holding it) and, astonishingly, when it sees another individual performing a similar action.' Humans have an even more elaborate mirror neuron system."


解题思路：C段提到相比猴子，人类拥有更复杂的镜像神经元系统mirror neuron system，因此答案是C。
31

31 the first discovery of brain activity related to mirror neurons
✅ 正确答案：D

定位信息：first discovery, brain activity, mirror neurons


对应原文：D段，" This phenomenon of neural mirroring was first discovered in 1954, when two French physiologists, Gastaut and Bert, found that the brains of humans vibrate with two distinct wavelengths, alpha and mu. The mu system is involved in neural mirroring. It is active when our bodies are still, and disappears whenever we do something active, like playing sport or changing the TV channel."


解题思路：D段提到1954年由两位法国生理学家Gastaut和Bert首次发现大脑产生mu波与模仿神经元相关，因此答案是D。
32

32 a claim linking observation to improvement in performance
✅ 正确答案：E

定位信息：observation, improvement, performance


对应原文：E段，"It turns out that watching a great golfer makes us better golfers, and watching a great sprinter actually makes us run faster."


解题思路：E段提到观察优秀运动员能提高自己的运动表现，因此答案是E。

Questions 33 - 35


Choose the correct answer.

33

The writer uses the term 'grammar of movement' to mean

正确答案： C. a pattern of connected cells.

定位信息：grammar of movement


对应原文：B段，"They discovered that inside these primate brains there were groups of cells that 'store vocabularies of motor actions'. Just as there are grammars of language, rules for forming a sentence, there are grammars of movement. These networks of cells are the bodily 'sentences' we use every day, the ones our brain has chosen to retain and refine. "


解题思路：B段将细胞和语言/句子进行类比，“grammar of movement”指的是储存动作模式的细胞网络，因此答案是C。


34

The writer states that expert players perform their actions

正确答案： A. without conscious thought.

定位信息：expert players


对应原文：B段，" To the expert, on the other hand, a perfectly balanced stroke is second nature. For him, the motor action has become memorised, and the movements are embedded in the neurons of his premotor cortex. He hits the ball with the tranquillity of his unconscious, his neurons on a perfected autopilot. "


解题思路：B段提到职业运动员控制挥杆已经变为了一种本能，神经潜意识地控制身体挥杆击球，因此答案是A。


35

The writer states that the most common motive for watching sport is to

正确答案： C. experience strong positive emotions.

定位信息：most common motive, watching sport


对应原文：F段，"But how many of us regularly watch sports in order to be a better athlete? Rather, we watch sport for the feeling, the human drama."


解题思路：F段提到我们观看比赛并不是是为了成为更优秀地运动员，而是为了感受人生的戏剧性，因此答案是C。

Questions 36 - 40


Choose YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer, choose NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer, or choose NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this.

36

Inexpert sports players are too aware of what they are doing.

正确答案： A. YES

定位信息：inexpert sports players, too aware


对应原文：B段，" To the novice, however, the skill of casting a smooth arc with a lop-sided metal stick is virtually impossible. This is because most novices swing with their consciousness, using an area of brain next to the premotor cortex."


解题思路：B段提到初学者需要刻意使用大脑控制挥杆动作，因此答案是YES。


37

Monkeys have a more complex mirror neuron system than humans.

正确答案： B. NO

定位信息：monkeys, more complex, mirror neuron system


对应原文：C段，" They note, 'The main functional characteristic of mirror neurons is that they become active both when the monkey performs a particular action (for example, grasping an object or holding it) and, astonishingly, when it sees another individual performing a similar action.' Humans have an even more elaborate mirror neuron system."


解题思路：C段明确表示相比猴子，人类的mirror neuron系统更复杂，因此答案是NO。


38

Looking at a photograph can activate mirror neurons.

正确答案： B. NO

定位信息：photograph, activate


对应原文：C段，"If we are staring at a photograph, a fixed image of a runner mid-stride, our mirror neurons are totally silent. They only fire when the runner is active: running, moving or sprinting."


解题思路：C段提到镜像神经元在观看照片时是静止的，只有在观看运动时这些神经元才处于激活状态，因此答案是NO。

39

Gastaut and Bert were both researchers and sports players.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

定位信息：Gastaut, Bert, researchers, sports players


对应原文：The passage mentions them as physiologists, but it does not mention if they were also sports players.


解题思路：原文只提到Gastaut,和Bert是神经学家但未提及他们是否是运动员，因此答案是NOT GIVEN。

40

The mu system is at rest when we are engaged in an activity.

正确答案： A. YES

定位信息：mu system, at rest

对应原文：D段，"The mu system is involved in neural mirroring. It is active when our bodies are still, and disappears whenever we do something active, like playing sport or changing the TV channel.The surprising fact is that the mu signal is also quiet when we watch someone else being active, as on TV."


解题思路：D段落提到mu系统当我们运动或者观看别人活动时都是不活跃地，因此答案是YES。
